attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a four-day history of increasing pain in his left hand. The pain has worsened to the point of being unbearable despite paracetamol and ibuprofen. He describes a history of a recent puncture wound to the palm of his hand while using a sharp instrument at his workbench. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is fusiform swelling of the digits of the left hand, and the fingers are held in slight flexion. Any attempt to passively move his fingers is limited by pain.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to commencing antibiotics, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic signs of infectious flexor tenosynovitis secondary to a penetrating injury to the palm. The condition is characterised by four cardinal signs: tenderness along the flexor sheath, digits held in slight flexion, pain on passive extension, and swelling of the digits. These are all present in this case.

Incision and drainage of the tendon sheath is the most appropriate next step in management. This can either be done as a closed flexor sheath catheter irrigation or open flexor sheath irrigation and debridement. This procedure is essential and urgent in cases of infectious flexor tenosynovitis. It allows for decompression of the tendon sheath and removal of purulent material, which is crucial for preventing further tissue damage and promoting healing. Antibiotics should also be administered, but surgical intervention is the primary treatment.

Blood cultures may be considered if there are systemic signs of infection, such as high fever or signs of sepsis. However, in this case, the patient's temperature is only slightly elevated, and blood cultures are not as urgent as surgical intervention.

Aspiration is not an appropriate treatment for infectious flexor tenosynovitis. It may provide a sample for culture but does not adequately decompress the tendon sheath or remove all the infected material.

Elevation of the hand might help reduce swelling in some hand conditions, but it is not a definitive treatment for infectious flexor tenosynovitis and will not provide significant relief in this acute situation.

A splint is not beneficial in this case and may actually worsen the pain by restricting movement. In some hand injuries, splinting can be helpful, but in infectious flexor tenosynovitis, it does not address the underlying infection and could potentially lead to further complications by delaying proper treatment.

It's important to note that infectious flexor tenosynovitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis. While imaging studies like ultrasound or MRI can be helpful in some cases, they are not necessary for diagnosis when the clinical presentation is clear, as in this case. The priority is to proceed with urgent surgical intervention to prevent further complications such as tissue necrosis or permanent loss of hand function.
learningPoints: Infectious flexor tenosynovitis is characterised by tenderness along the flexor sheath, digits held in slight flexion, pain on passive extension, and swelling of the digits.
Infectious flexor tenosynovitis is a clinical diagnosis and does not require further investigations before treatment.
Urgent incision and drainage of the tendon sheath is essential in the management of infectious flexor tenosynovitis.
references: Ray G, Sandean DP, Tall MA. Tenosynovitis. In: StatPearls [Online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK544324/
